verses.text: The law begins not with retribution but with restitution. A thief restores five-for-one or four-for-one — the victim is made more than whole. Ancient Near Eastern codes more often executed thieves; Israel’s law instead values the loss and protects life. The principle here, the Hebrew shillem, is to make whole. Justice in Torah leans toward repair rather than revenge.

A specific case: if a thief is caught breaking in at night, in the confusion of self-defense, the household isn’t guilty. The law recognises that fear distorts what people see in the dark. Compare verse 3: by daylight the same act becomes blood-guilt. Torah is finely calibrated to context — the moral weight of an action depends on what could reasonably be known.

By daylight the thief should be visible enough to be captured rather than killed. If he cannot repay, he himself is sold — a hard mercy, since indentured service was time-limited under Israelite law (Ex 21:2) and not the slavery of surrounding nations. The verse aims at restitution to the victim rather than punishment of the offender, even when poverty makes the path costly.

If the stolen animal is found alive, double restitution. The principle: a thief pays back exactly what he tried to gain. He wanted one beast unearned; now he gives up one of his own to match it. The math is precise so the moral weight is unmistakable. Justice is not arbitrary anger but proportionate repair.

Letting your livestock graze in a neighbour’s field counts as theft — even if no fence was crossed deliberately. The restitution comes from ‘the best of his own field, and of the best of his own vineyard.’ You cannot offset careless damage with whatever’s leftover. Love of neighbour, in Torah, includes paying for the harm your inattention causes.

Fire that escapes a controlled burn obligates the one who lit it. Pre-modern fields cleared by burning could destroy a year’s harvest in an hour. The law assigns responsibility to the source. Negligence is taken seriously in Torah — sin is not only what you intend but what your carelessness sets loose. The principle echoes through proverbs and James 3 on the tongue.

When someone safeguards a neighbour’s goods and they’re stolen, the thief (when found) pays double. The point: hospitality and trust are protected, but the actual wrong is traced to the actual wrongdoer. The custodian is not punished for what someone else did. Torah’s instinct here is meticulous fairness — each party carries only the weight they earned.

If the thief isn’t found, the trusted custodian must come ‘before the judges’ — elohim in Hebrew, sometimes translated ‘God’ and sometimes ‘judges,’ reflecting that earthly verdicts represent divine judgment. The court tests whether the custodian himself took the goods. Without forensic evidence, the case turns to oath and conscience: human courts pointing upward to the One who sees what they cannot.

A general principle for contested ownership: both parties bring their case to the judges, and whoever is found liable pays double. The doubling, again, makes false claims costly. Torah’s ordinary case-law assumes neighbours will disagree; the question is whether disagreement is settled by force, by deception, or by community-sanctioned verdict. Israel is being trained in the slow patience of justice.

Entrusting an animal to a neighbour for safekeeping covered situations where pasture was thin or travel necessary. If something happens without witnesses, the law turns to oath rather than evidence (verse 11). The verse acknowledges what every herder knew: animals die, run off, or are taken, and not every misfortune is fraud. Torah builds compassion into its forensics.

An oath ‘between them both’ — before the Lord — settles what evidence cannot. The Hebrew shevu‘ah carries weight because it puts the swearer’s own integrity on the line, with God as witness. The owner accepts the loss and lets it go. Israel is taught that some disputes end not in a clean verdict but in trust and the fear of God.

But if the animal was stolen (not lost), the keeper is liable — he has failed his charge. The law expects diligence proportionate to the responsibility accepted. Holding what isn’t ours obliges us to its care. This quiet principle ripples through Scripture: from stewardship of gardens (Gen 2:15) to Jesus’s parables of entrusted talents.

If predators kill the animal, the keeper brings the remains as evidence and owes nothing. Torah accommodates the realities of pastoral life — lions and wolves were real; not every loss is failure. The detail of ‘bringing it for witness’ shows the law’s humanity: honest accounting is enough; perfection is not demanded.

Borrowing carries higher liability than safekeeping. If you ask to use what isn’t yours and it’s damaged in your hands, you replace it. The asymmetry is gentle: when we receive something as a favour, we owe the lender our care. James 1:17 will echo this Torah instinct — every good gift comes with the dignity of stewardship.

Unless the owner was present, in which case the loss falls to him — he was there. Or if the animal was rented, the rental fee already covered the risk. Tiny distinctions, but they reveal how Torah works: not by sweeping rules but by careful attention to who knew what and who had power. Love of neighbour requires that kind of precision.

Estos casos variados que involucran la responsabilidad social no están categorizados. Todas las partes de la vida son una expresión de la obediencia a Dios (ver nota de estudio sobre 21:1–23:19 ).\n\n

Los hebreos debían tratar a los extranjeros con amabilidad porque ellos también habían sido extranjeros (ver también **23:9** , **Dt 10:18-19** ). Para un mayor desarrollo del principio, ver **Lucas 6:31** .\n\n

Debido al significado simbólico de la sangre como la vida misma, Dios prohibió comer carne con la sangre aún en ella (ver **Lv 17:10-16** ).\n\n
